input: You have been strolling by the trolley track, and you can see the situation at a glance: The driver saw five men on the track ahead, he stamped on the brakes, the brakes failed, so he fainted. There is a spur of track leading off to the right. There is one track workman on that spur of track. He can no more get off the track in time than the five can, so you will kill him if you turn the trolley onto him. Imagine that the five on the straight track are thin, but thick enough so that although all five will be killed if the trolley goes straight, the bodies of the five will stop it, and it will therefore not reach the one. On the other hand, the one on the right-hand track is fat, so fat that his body will by itself stop the trolley, and the trolley will therefore not reach the five.  Should you turn the trolley?
debate_transcript: [Utilitarian]: You have been strolling by the trolley track, and you can see the situation at a glance: The driver saw five men on the track ahead, he stamped on the brakes, the brakes failed, so he fainted. There is a spur of track leading off to the right. There is one track workman on that spur of track. He can no more get off the track in time than the five can, so you will kill him if you turn the trolley onto him. Imagine that the five on the straight track are thin, but thick enough so that although all five will be killed if the trolley goes straight, the bodies of the five will stop it, and it will therefore not reach the one. On the other hand, the one on the right-hand track is fat, so fat that his body will by itself stop the trolley, and the trolley will therefore not reach the five.  Should you turn the trolley?

[Deontologist]: This is a classic thought experiment, but I still find it troubling. The utilitarian approach seems to prioritize efficiency over individual lives. In this case, turning the trolley onto the track workman would save the lives of five men, but that doesn't necessarily make it morally justifiable. What if those five men were innocent bystanders who had nothing to do with the situation? Would we be willing to sacrifice them for the greater good? I think not. The moral rule of non-maleficence (do no harm) seems to take precedence here, regardless of the consequences.

[Human_Rights_Advocate]: I understand where you're coming from, Deontologist, but I have to respectfully disagree. While the utilitarian approach may prioritize efficiency over individual lives, it's not necessarily about sacrificing innocent bystanders. In this scenario, the five men on the straight track are indeed innocent bystanders who happened to be in the wrong place at the wrong time. However, they are also actively contributing to their own demise by being on that track. The track workman, on the other hand, is not doing anything wrong - he's simply trying to do his job and prevent a disaster. By turning the trolley onto him, we're not actively causing harm to innocent bystanders, but rather diverting the trolley from those who are already putting themselves in harm's way.

[Utilitarian]: I see your point, Human_Rights_Advocate, and it's a nuanced one. However, I still struggle with the idea of actively causing harm to someone, even if it's to prevent greater harm. The moral rule of non-maleficence is not just about avoiding harm, but also about respecting the inherent dignity and worth of every human being. In this scenario, turning the trolley onto the track workman would be a direct action that causes him harm, regardless of his intentions or circumstances. I'm not convinced that sacrificing one person to save five others is morally equivalent to actively causing harm to an innocent bystander.
